movements.name: Shoulder CARs
movements.notes: Controlled articular rotations - slow, deliberate shoulder circles through full ROM

movements.name: Banded Pull-Apart
movements.notes: Light band, focus on scapular retraction

movements.name: Inchworm
movements.notes: Walk hands out to plank, walk feet to hands

movements.name: Empty Bar Strict Press
movements.notes: Movement-specific prep, focus on bar path

strength.label: Strict Press — Ramp to Heavy Double

movements.name: Strict Press
movements.notes: Progressive loading scheme. Bar path must be vertical. If 1RM unknown, build to RPE 9 on final double.

movements.name: Push Press
movements.notes: Use leg drive, catch in partial dip
movements.scaling: Reduce load to maintain unbroken sets

movements.name: Pull-Up
movements.notes: Strict or kipping, chest to bar not required

movements.name: Burpee
movements.notes: Chest and hips to ground, full hip extension at top

movements.name: World's Greatest Stretch
movements.notes: Hip flexor, thoracic rotation, hamstring stretch combo

movements.name: Foam Roll — Lats
movements.notes: Target latissimus from armpit to mid-back

session_why.text: Vertical pressing strength is placed first to exploit full CNS availability. The metcon uses moderate-load pressing paired with pulling to maintain movement balance (push:pull ratio 1:1.5 favoring pull) and targets the glycolytic system with 12-minute AMRAP duration.
session_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed, Ch. 17; Glassman 2002 - CrossFit Journal

movement_why.text: Strict press is the foundation for all overhead strength. Ramp loading allows progressive neural preparation under increasing load. Push press in metcon uses previously fatigued pressing musculature at moderate intensity, training strength-endurance. Pull-ups provide antagonist balance.
movement_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training, Ch. 3; CrossFit Level 2 Training Guide

loading_why.text: Ramp/buildup scheme (5→4→3→2 reps at 65%→85%) prepares the nervous system for heavier loads while developing technique under progressively challenging weights. This is appropriate for Week 1 baseline establishment or complex movement introduction. Metcon load kept moderate (95/65 lb) to allow volume accumulation without technical breakdown.
loading_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training; Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed

movements.name: Band Pass-Through
movements.notes: Use resistance band, wide grip, open shoulders

movements.name: Thoracic Rotation
movements.notes: Quadruped position, hand behind head, rotate through mid-back

movements.name: Ring Row
movements.notes: Body angle to allow controlled reps

movements.name: Empty Bar Bench Press
movements.notes: Movement prep, touch chest, full lockout

strength.label: Bench Press — E2MOM Build + Chest-to-Bar Skill

movements.name: Bench Press
movements.loading: E2MOM x 8: Build from 70% to 80% 1RM (or RPE 7→8)
movements.notes: Auto-regulated rest. If reps take 30 sec, you rest 90 sec. Build load across rounds: start 70%, finish 80%.

movements.name: Chest-to-Bar Pull-Up Skill Work
movements.notes: Focus on pulling chest to bar, not just chin over. If cannot perform strict, use band assistance. Scaling: strict pull-up to chest height.

movements.name: Pull-Up
movements.scaling: Banded pull-up or ring row x 15

session_why.text: Horizontal pressing (bench press) is a foundational upper-body strength pattern. The EMOM format maintains session flow and rewards efficient execution. Chest-to-bar skill work develops vertical pulling proficiency. The metcon uses bodyweight push/pull movements plus monostructural cardio, targeting glycolytic and oxidative systems with 5-round structure (~10 min duration).
session_why.source: Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed; Gastin 2001 - Energy System Interaction

movement_why.text: Bench press and chest-to-bar pull-up together provide balanced horizontal push and vertical pull volume. Push:pull ratio is 1:1 in strength block (4 sets bench, 4 sets C2B) and maintained in metcon. This prevents shoulder imbalance and supports long-term joint health.
movement_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed; CrossFit Level 2 Training Guide

loading_why.text: E2MOM format with progressive loading (70%→80%) builds work capacity at moderate-heavy intensity while maintaining pacing discipline. The 2-minute interval provides adequate phosphocreatine recovery for 4-rep sets at these intensities. Metcon kept to bodyweight movements to avoid excessive loading after heavy bench work.
loading_why.source: Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed; Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed

movements.name: Shoulder CARs
movements.notes: Controlled articular rotations - prepare shoulder for ring instability

movements.name: Ring Row
movements.notes: Body angle progression: start easy, finish challenging

movements.name: Ring Support Hold
movements.notes: Active shoulders, stable rings, hollow body tension

strength.label: Ring Dip Strength + Hollow Body Foundation

movements.name: Ring Dip
movements.loading: Straight sets at RPE 7-8 (or 70-75% max reps)
movements.notes: If cannot perform 5 strict ring dips, scale to parallel bar dip or box dip. Maintain ring stability throughout.
movements.tempo: 3 sec eccentric, 1 sec pause at bottom, explosive up

movements.name: Hollow Body Hold
movements.notes: Lower back pressed to floor, arms overhead, legs straight and low. If form breaks, bend knees to maintain position.

movements.name: Arch Body Hold
movements.notes: Opposite of hollow - chest and thighs off ground, arms and legs extended. Alternate with hollow holds.

movements.name: Pull-Up
movements.scaling: Banded pull-up or ring row x 12

movements.name: Burpee
movements.notes: Min 4, 8, 12: Fast singles or small sets

metcon.target_score: Complete all reps within each minute with 10-15 sec rest

movements.name: Pigeon Pose
movements.notes: Hip external rotation and hip flexor stretch

session_why.text: Ring dips require high shoulder stability and are foundational for ring muscle-up progression. Hollow and arch body holds develop the body tension prerequisite for all gymnastics movements. The EMOM metcon maintains intensity while allowing brief recovery each minute, targeting the glycolytic system with 12-minute duration.
session_why.source: Sommer - Building the Gymnastic Body; CrossFit Gymnastics Specialty Course; Gastin 2001 - Energy System Interaction

movement_why.text: Ring dips develop vertical pushing strength under unstable conditions, requiring greater stabilizer recruitment than parallel bar dips. Hollow and arch holds are non-negotiable foundations per Sommer's gymnastics progression model. The metcon balances push (push-ups, burpees) and pull (pull-ups, row) at 1:1 ratio.
movement_why.source: Sommer - Building the Gymnastic Body; CrossFit Gymnastics Specialty Course

loading_why.text: Straight sets at RPE 7-8 (5x5 ring dips) build baseline strength-endurance in the movement without excessive CNS fatigue. This loading is appropriate for Week 1-2 baseline phases or when introducing unstable surface work. Hollow/arch holds use fixed 30-sec duration to develop isometric endurance.
loading_why.source: Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed; Sommer - Building the Gymnastic Body

movements.name: Band Pass-Through
movements.notes: Wide grip, open shoulders and chest

movements.name: Hip Circles
movements.notes: Leg extended, draw circles with foot

movements.name: Empty Bar Push Press
movements.notes: Dip, drive, press - establish bar path

strength.label: Push Press — Top Set + Back-Off Volume

movements.name: Push Press
movements.loading: Top set: 3 reps @ 85% 1RM (or RPE 8-9). Back-off: 4 x 6 @ 70% 1RM
movements.rest: 3 min after top set, then 90 sec between back-off sets
movements.notes: Top set + back-off scheme: neural stimulation (heavy triple) followed by hypertrophy volume (4x6 at lighter load). If 1RM unknown, build to RPE 8-9 on triple, then reduce ~15-20% for back-off sets.

movements.name: Chest-to-Bar Pull-Up
movements.notes: Kipping or strict, chest must touch bar
movements.scaling: Regular pull-up x 15 or banded C2B x 12

movements.name: World's Greatest Stretch
movements.notes: Hip flexor, hamstring, thoracic rotation combo

movements.name: Foam Roll — Lats
movements.notes: Armpit to mid-back, pause on tender spots

session_why.text: Push press combines pressing strength with lower body drive, developing power and work capacity. The top set + back-off volume scheme allows peak intensity exposure without excessive CNS fatigue, followed by structural hypertrophy work. The metcon emphasizes vertical pulling capacity (C2B) balanced against horizontal pushing (push-ups) with monostructural cardio intervals.
session_why.source: Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed, Ch. 9; Glassman 2002 - CrossFit Journal

movement_why.text: Push press is a hybrid movement training the press pattern with explosive leg drive. C2B pull-ups develop vertical pulling strength beyond standard pull-ups. Push:pull ratio in metcon is 1:1 (20 push-ups : 12 C2B adjusted for difficulty). Assault bike provides glycolytic stress without additional upper-body loading.
movement_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed; CrossFit Level 2 Training Guide

loading_why.text: Top set + back-off volume scheme (1x3@85%, 4x6@70%) exploits neural priming from heavy loading (post-activation potentiation) while accumulating hypertrophy volume at moderate intensity. This is efficient for concurrent training and appropriate for Week 3 peak intensity sessions. The heavy top set happens fresh; volume work follows with reduced load.
loading_why.source: Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed, Ch. 9; Robbins 2005 - Post-Activation Potentiation

movements.name: Thoracic Rotation
movements.notes: Quadruped, hand behind head, rotate through mid-back

movements.name: Inchworm
movements.notes: Walk out to plank, walk feet to hands

movements.name: Good Morning (empty bar)
movements.notes: Hip hinge pattern, posterior chain activation

movements.name: Empty Bar Barbell Row
movements.notes: Hip hinge position, pull to sternum

strength.label: Barbell Row — Wave Loading + HSPU Progression

movements.name: Barbell Row (Bent Over)
movements.notes: Wave loading: oscillate between moderate and heavy loads. Maintain hip hinge position throughout. Pull to lower sternum, elbows back. If 1RM unknown, use RPE and build waves.

movements.name: Handstand Push-Up (or progression)
movements.notes: If cannot perform strict HSPU, scale to: deficit pike push-up, box pike push-up, or wall-facing HSPU with abmat. Focus on vertical torso and full lockout.

movements.name: Ring Row
movements.notes: Body angle challenging but sustainable, pull chest to rings

movements.name: Row
movements.notes: Target 80% effort, focus on pull through legs and back

session_why.text: Barbell rows develop horizontal pulling strength, often undertrained relative to vertical pulling. HSPU skill work addresses vertical pressing in an inverted position. The metcon maintains push:pull balance (10 push-ups : 15 ring rows, adjusted for difficulty) and includes monostructural rowing to target oxidative system.
session_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed; CrossFit Gymnastics Specialty Course

movement_why.text: Barbell rows target the posterior chain (lats, rhomboids, traps) with heavy loading potential. Wave loading exploits post-activation potentiation by alternating heavy and moderate loads. HSPU (or progressions) develop vertical pressing in a gymnastics context, requiring shoulder stability and core tension.
movement_why.source: Robbins 2005 - Post-Activation Potentiation; Sommer - Building the Gymnastic Body

loading_why.text: Wave loading (72%→77%→82%→72%→80%→85%) allows volume accumulation at high intensity by oscillating load. Heavy sets prime the nervous system for subsequent performance. This scheme is appropriate for Week 2-3 strength emphasis with experienced athletes. The waves prevent linear fatigue accumulation while maintaining intensity.
loading_why.source: Robbins 2005 - Post-Activation Potentiation; Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed

movements.name: Hollow Body Hold
movements.notes: Lower back pressed to floor, arms overhead, legs straight and low. Non-negotiable gymnastics foundation. Scale: bend knees if form breaks.

movements.name: Arch Body Hold
movements.notes: Chest and thighs off ground, arms and legs extended. Build to 30 sec without compensation.

movements.name: Pull-Up (strict or kipping)
movements.notes: Work:rest ratio ~1:3. Goal is total volume, not singles. If strict, aim for 3-8 reps. If kipping, 8-15 reps per set. Scale: banded pull-up or ring row.

movements.name: Burpee
movements.notes: Chest and hips to ground, jump at top

movements.name: Ring Row
movements.notes: Body angle sustainable for 12 reps unbroken

movements.name: Double-Under
movements.notes: Singles x 90 if cannot perform double-unders

movements.name: Foam Roll — Lats
movements.notes: Armpit to mid-back, pause on tender areas

session_why.text: Hollow and arch body holds are foundational for all gymnastics movements per Sommer's progression model. High-volume pull-up work (5x30sec max effort sets) develops muscular endurance and work capacity. The metcon uses bodyweight movements only, targeting muscular endurance and glycolytic system with 12-minute AMRAP.
session_why.source: Sommer - Building the Gymnastic Body; CrossFit Gymnastics Specialty Course; Gastin 2001 - Energy System Interaction

movement_why.text: Hollow and arch holds are non-negotiable prerequisites for kipping, muscle-ups, and handstand work. Pull-up volume builds pulling capacity. Metcon maintains push:pull balance (10 push-ups + 8 burpees : 12 ring rows, ~1:1 ratio). Double-unders add monostructural conditioning without upper-body load.
movement_why.source: Sommer - Building the Gymnastic Body; Glassman 2002 - CrossFit Journal

loading_why.text: Max rep sets in fixed time (30 sec work, 90 sec rest) create a 1:3 work:rest ratio appropriate for strength-endurance. This develops work capacity without compromising movement quality. Straight sets at bodyweight for hollow/arch holds build isometric endurance progressively (5x30 sec).
loading_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed; Sommer - Building the Gymnastic Body

strength.label: Press + Push Jerk Complex — E3MOM Build

movements.name: Strict Press + Push Jerk Complex
movements.reps: 2 Strict Press + 1 Push Jerk per round
movements.loading: E3MOM x 7: Build from 65% to 80% 1RM (or RPE 7→8)
movements.notes: Perform 2 strict presses, then 1 push jerk without dropping the bar. Build load across rounds. E3MOM provides full PCr recovery for each complex. If 1RM unknown, start at RPE 6-7 and build to RPE 8.

movements.name: Push Jerk
movements.notes: Use leg drive, catch in partial squat. Should be unbroken or small sets.
movements.scaling: Reduce to 75/55 lb or push press

movements.name: Pull-Up
movements.notes: Kipping or strict, chin over bar
movements.scaling: Banded pull-up or ring row (add 6 reps: 27-21-15)

movements.name: Ski Erg
movements.notes: Pull through lats and core, hinge at hips
movements.scaling: Reduce to 18-12-6 cal or row same calories

session_why.text: Combining strict press with push jerk in a complex taxes the pressing musculature under different loading conditions (grinding strength vs. explosive power). The E3MOM format provides full phosphocreatine recovery (3 min) appropriate for heavy complexes. The 21-15-9 metcon structure creates a descending rep scheme targeting glycolytic system with ~10 min duration.
session_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed; Glassman 2002 - CrossFit Journal

movement_why.text: Strict press develops pure pressing strength; push jerk adds explosive power and leg drive. Pairing them without dropping the bar increases time under tension. Metcon maintains push:pull ratio (push jerk : pull-up 1:1) and includes ski erg for upper-body dominant monostructural work.
movement_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training; CrossFit Level 2 Training Guide

loading_why.text: E3MOM with progressive loading (65%→80%) allows gradual intensity increase while maintaining quality. The 3-minute interval is necessary for complex work requiring multiple reps per round. Building across 7 rounds accumulates volume (14 presses + 7 jerks) at increasing intensity. This is appropriate for Week 2 baseline building or complex movement introduction.
loading_why.source: Bompa & Haff - Periodization 5th ed; Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed
